flashcards.front: What is a Distributed File System (DFS)?
flashcards.back: A file system that allows access to files from multiple hosts across a network, providing a unified and transparent view of files stored on various servers.

flashcards.front: What is the role of the Directory Service?
flashcards.back: It maps human-readable pathnames (e.g., /home/user/file.txt) to machine-readable Unique File Identifiers (UFIDs) used by the Flat File Service.
A design goal where users access files without needing to know their physical location (e.g., which server or disk they reside on).

flashcards.front: What is the key difference between Unix File Semantics and Session Semantics?
flashcards.back: Unix Semantics ensure every read sees all previous writes (strong consistency). Session Semantics make a client's writes visible to others only upon file close, favoring performance.

flashcards.front: Why is the stateless design of an NFS server beneficial?
flashcards.back: It simplifies crash recovery. A failed server, upon restarting, does not need to recover any client state. Clients can simply retry their requests.
